metadata.description: Master MIT LLM/AI Agents UROP Database

part1_professor_database.name: Jacob Andreas
part1_professor_database.title: Associate Professor, EECS

research_groups.name: LINGO (Language & Intelligence) Group
research_groups.focus: LLM reasoning, in-context learning, language-guided planning, multimodal interpretability

part1_professor_database.research_summary: Leads MIT's most prolific LLM lab. Work spans LLM reasoning and evaluation, self-steering language models, in-context learning, multimodal interpretability agents, and language-guided robot planning. Extensive collaborations with Tenenbaum, Kim, Levy, Torralba.
key_papers_2024_2025: DisCIPL: Self-Steering Language Models (compositional inference with LLMs)
Breakpoint: Evaluating LLM agents via environment perturbations
MAIA: Multimodal Automated Interpretability Agent (ICML 2024)
Reasoning or Reciting? Capabilities of LLMs through counterfactual evals (NAACL 2024)
The Consensus Game: LM generation via equilibrium search (ICLR 2024 Spotlight)
OpenEstimate: Evaluating LLMs on reasoning under uncertainty (ICLR 2026)
Log-Augmented Generation: scaling test-time reasoning (ICLR 2026)
Learning Linear Attention in Polynomial Time (NeurIPS 2025 Oral)
A spectral understanding of LoRA fine-tuning (NeurIPS 2025)
EWoK: Evaluating basic world knowledge in LLMs
Eliciting Human Preferences with Language Models (ICLR 2025)
Test-time training for few-shot learning (ICML 2025)
Beyond binary rewards: training LMs to reason about uncertainty
Generative AI in the Era of Alternative Facts (EMNLP 2024)
LaMPP: Language Models as Probabilistic Priors for Perception and Action
Visual grounding helps learn word meanings (NAACL 2024 Best Paper)
Whale communication analysis with LLMs (Nature Communications 2024)
part1_professor_database.urop_relevance: High - large lab with active undergraduate involvement; co-leads SQI Language and Thought Mission which offers LLM UROPs

part1_professor_database.name: Yoon Kim
part1_professor_database.title: Associate Professor, EECS (tenured effective July 2025)

research_groups.name: Yoon Kim's NLP Group
research_groups.focus: LLM architectures, efficient transformers, self-adapting LLMs, language model analysis

part1_professor_database.research_summary: Core LLM architecture researcher. Works on efficient LLM architectures (gated linear attention), the semantic hub hypothesis (how LLMs share representations across languages), self-adapting LLMs (SEAL), Co-LLM collaborative decoding. Co-teaches NLP with Andreas.
key_papers_2024_2025: SEAL: Self-Adapting LLMs via RL-based self-editing (NeurIPS, with UROP co-author)
Semantic Hub Hypothesis: Language models share semantic representations across languages and modalities
Co-LLM: collaborative decoding combining general and specialized LLMs
Gated Linear Attention Transformers: efficient alternative to softmax attention
Reasoning or Reciting? (NAACL 2024, with Andreas)
In-context language learning: Architectures and algorithms (with Andreas)
Beyond binary rewards: training LMs to reason about uncertainty
part1_professor_database.urop_relevance: High - SEAL paper had a UROP co-author funded by Stevens Fund

part1_professor_database.name: Omar Khattab
part1_professor_database.title: Assistant Professor, EECS (joined Fall 2025)

research_groups.name: NLP@MIT (Khattab Lab)
research_groups.focus: LLM programming, retrieval-augmented generation, declarative AI systems, language model compilers

part1_professor_database.research_summary: Creator of DSPy (first LLM program compiler, 500K+ monthly downloads) and ColBERT retrieval model. Pioneer of retrieval-augmented generation. Research on composing retrieval and reasoning, automated RAG evaluation (ARES), and LLM programming abstractions. One of MIT's newest and most directly LLM-focused hires - building lab now.
key_papers_2024_2025: DSPy: Compiling Declarative Language Model Calls into Self-Improving Pipelines (ICLR 2024 Spotlight)
DSPy Assertions: Computational Constraints for Self-Refining LM Pipelines
STORM: Writing Wikipedia-like Articles from Scratch with LLMs (NAACL 2024)
ARES: Automated Evaluation Framework for RAG Systems (NAACL 2024)
PAPILLON: Privacy Preservation from LLM Ensembles (NAACL 2025)
GEPA: Reflective Prompt Evolution Can Outperform RL (2025)
ColBERT-serve: Efficient Multi-Stage Memory-Mapped Scoring (ECIR 2025)
WARP: Scalable Retrieval Infrastructure (SIGIR 2025 Best Paper)
FreshStack: Building Realistic Benchmarks for Retrieval on Technical Documents
Grounding by Trying: LLMs with RL-Enhanced Retrieval (ICLR 2025)
PATH and IReRa: state-of-the-art LM pipeline systems
MIPRO: Multi-prompt Instruction Proposal Optimizer (EMNLP 2024)
BetterTogether: improving LM pipelines (EMNLP 2024)
part1_professor_database.urop_relevance: VERY HIGH - brand new lab, actively recruiting, most likely to need UROPs

part1_professor_database.name: Song Han
part1_professor_database.title: Associate Professor (with tenure), EECS

research_groups.name: MIT HAN Lab
research_groups.focus: Efficient LLM inference, quantization, long-context LLMs, on-device AI, vision-language models

part1_professor_database.research_summary: Leading researcher on efficient LLM inference and deployment. AWQ (19M+ HuggingFace downloads, MLSys 2024 Best Paper), SmoothQuant (W8A8 quantization), StreamingLLM (infinite-length generation), DuoAttention, QServe, LServe, VILA vision-language models. Methods integrated into NVIDIA TensorRT-LLM.
key_papers_2024_2025: AWQ: Activation-aware Weight Quantization for LLM Compression (MLSys 2024 Best Paper)
SmoothQuant: Accurate and Efficient Post-Training Quantization for LLMs (ICML 2023)
StreamingLLM: Efficient Streaming LM with Attention Sinks
StreamingVLM: streaming-aware KV cache for infinite video understanding
QServe: W4A8KV4 Quantization for large-scale LLM Serving
LServe: Efficient Long-sequence LLM Serving
DuoAttention: reducing LLM decoding/pre-filling memory with retrieval/streaming heads
Quest: query-aware sparsity in long-context KV cache
COAT: Memory efficient FP8 training
VILA: Vision-Language Models on the edge with AWQ
DistriFusion: multi-GPU diffusion model acceleration (CVPR 2024 Highlight)
VLaSH: Real-Time VLAs via Future-state-aware Asynchronous Inference
Four Over Six: NVFP4 Quantization with Adaptive Block Scaling
Radial Attention: O(nlogn) Sparse Attention for Long Video Generation (NeurIPS 2025)
part1_professor_database.urop_relevance: High - large active lab with many students

part1_professor_database.name: Armando Solar-Lezama
part1_professor_database.title: Distinguished Professor of Computing; CSAIL Associate Director & COO

research_groups.name: Computer-Aided Programming Group
research_groups.focus: Program synthesis, LLMs for code generation, neurosymbolic programming, AI agents for software engineering

part1_professor_database.research_summary: Intersection of program synthesis and AI. Extensive work on LLMs for code generation, neurosymbolic programming, and AI agents for software engineering. Developed EnCompass (AI agent search framework), combines LLMs with first-order logic provers (LINC).
key_papers_2024_2025: EnCompass: AI agent search framework for software engineering
LINC: Combining LLMs with First-Order Logic Provers (EMNLP 2023, with Levy)
Limits of LLMs for reasoning tasks
Neurosymbolic programming with language models
LLM-guided program repair and synthesis
part1_professor_database.urop_relevance: Medium-High - established lab

part1_professor_database.name: Tim Kraska
part1_professor_database.title: Associate Professor, EECS; College of Computing Distinguished Professor

research_groups.name: Data Systems and AI Lab (DSAIL)
research_groups.focus: Agentic AI for data systems, LLM-powered data management, generative AI for databases

part1_professor_database.research_summary: Co-directs MIT Generative AI Impact Consortium. Develops declarative frameworks for AI workloads (Palimpzest), created KramaBench (first benchmark for data-science agents), explores agentic systems with long-context optimization and graph-based reasoning.
key_papers_2024_2025: Palimpzest: Declarative framework for AI workloads
KramaBench: First benchmark for data-science agents
Long-context LLM optimization for agentic systems
Graph-based reasoning with language models
part1_professor_database.urop_relevance: High - leads MGAIC which supports undergraduate research

part1_professor_database.name: Pulkit Agrawal
part1_professor_database.title: Associate Professor, EECS

research_groups.name: Improbable AI Lab
research_groups.focus: Foundation models for robotics, self-adapting LLMs, robot manipulation

part1_professor_database.research_summary: Co-developed SEAL (Self-Adapting LLMs) with Yoon Kim, enabling LLMs to permanently learn from new inputs via RL-based self-editing. Also works on world models for robotics from human videos and foundation models for robotic manipulation.
key_papers_2024_2025: SEAL: Self-Adapting LLMs via RL-based self-editing (NeurIPS, UROP co-author)
World Models for Robotics from Human Videos
Foundation models for robotic manipulation
part1_professor_database.urop_relevance: VERY HIGH - SEAL paper included UROP student; has active UROP listed on ELX

part1_professor_database.name: Antonio Torralba
part1_professor_database.title: Delta Electronics Professor; Faculty Head of AI+D

research_groups.name: Torralba Lab (Vision Group)
research_groups.focus: Multimodal AI, vision-language models, automated interpretability, multi-agent debate for LLM factuality

part1_professor_database.research_summary: Multimodal AI pioneer. Published MAIA (Multimodal Automated Interpretability Agent), improving LLM factuality through multi-agent debate, vision check-ups for language models, and composable diffusion models. Active at intersection of vision-language foundation models.
key_papers_2024_2025: MAIA: Multimodal Automated Interpretability Agent (ICML 2024)
Multi-agent debate for improving LLM factuality
Vision check-ups for language models
Composable diffusion models
A spectral understanding of LoRA fine-tuning (NeurIPS 2025, with Andreas)
LucidSim: synthetic robot training data (with Isola)
part1_professor_database.urop_relevance: Medium - large established lab

part1_professor_database.name: Phillip Isola
part1_professor_database.title: Associate Professor, EECS

research_groups.name: Isola Lab
research_groups.focus: Generative AI, diffusion models, representation convergence across modalities, synthetic data for robotics

part1_professor_database.research_summary: Generative AI and the Platonic Representation Hypothesis (how multimodal models converge on shared representations). Uses generative AI to create synthetic robot training data (LucidSim). Research connects foundation models across vision and language modalities.
key_papers_2024_2025: The Platonic Representation Hypothesis (multimodal convergence)
LucidSim: synthetic robot training data with generative AI
Diffusion model advances for generation and perception
part1_professor_database.urop_relevance: Medium

part1_professor_database.name: Kaiming He
part1_professor_database.title: Associate Professor, EECS (joined February 2024)

research_groups.name: Kaiming He Lab
research_groups.focus: Visual foundation models, self-supervised learning, scalable representation learning

part1_professor_database.research_summary: Pioneering deep learning researcher (ResNet, Masked Autoencoders). Works on visual foundation models, self-supervised learning approaches parallel to LLM pre-training. Sponsors 'AI + Chaotic System' UROP using foundation models.
key_papers_2024_2025: Foundation models for chaotic systems and PDE solvers
Masked Autoencoders (MAE) extensions
Visual representation learning at scale
part1_professor_database.urop_relevance: HIGH - has active ELX UROP listing

part1_professor_database.name: Paul Pu Liang
part1_professor_database.title: Assistant Professor, Media Arts & Sciences and EECS (joined September 2024)

research_groups.name: Multisensory Intelligence Group
research_groups.focus: Multimodal AI, RAG-driven multimodal LLMs, social bias in language models, AI for healthcare

part1_professor_database.research_summary: Built theoretical foundations and large-scale resources for multimodal AI widely used in generative models. Published definitive survey on multimodal ML (Computing Surveys 2024). RAG-driven multimodal LLMs, social bias in language models. Forbes 30 Under 30. Teaches 'How to AI (Almost) Anything.'
key_papers_2024_2025: Foundations and Recent Trends in Multimodal Machine Learning (Computing Surveys 2024, definitive survey)
RAG-driven multimodal LLMs for ECG interpretation
Social bias in language models
Multimodal AI applications in healthcare and robotics
How to AI (Almost) Anything (course materials)
part1_professor_database.urop_relevance: VERY HIGH - brand new lab, actively recruiting

part1_professor_database.name: Alex Rives
part1_professor_database.title: Assistant Professor, EECS (joined September 2024)

research_groups.name: Rives Lab (Protein Language Models)
research_groups.focus: Protein language models, biological sequence generation, evolutionary-scale modeling

part1_professor_database.research_summary: Pioneer of protein language models. Founded ESM team at Meta. ESM3 (Science 2025) is a frontier generative language model that simulated 500M years of evolution and generated a novel fluorescent protein. Demonstrates how transformer architectures extend to biological sequences.
key_papers_2024_2025: ESM3: Simulating 500 million years of evolution with a language model (Science 2025)
ESM-2: Protein language model scaling laws
Biological sequence generation and protein design with LLMs
part1_professor_database.urop_relevance: HIGH - brand new lab, actively recruiting

part1_professor_database.name: Regina Barzilay
part1_professor_database.title: School of Engineering Distinguished Professor of AI & Health

research_groups.name: NLP Group (co-lead)
research_groups.focus: NLP for drug discovery, clinical AI, computational chemistry

research_groups.name: MIT Jameel Clinic
research_groups.focus: AI for healthcare

part1_professor_database.research_summary: Co-leads MIT CSAIL NLP Group. Pioneer in NLP applied to drug discovery, clinical AI, and computational chemistry. Foundational NLP work includes decipherment of dead languages, cross-lingual transfer.
key_papers_2024_2025: NLP for drug discovery and molecular generation
Clinical AI with language models
Cross-lingual and cross-domain NLP transfer
part1_professor_database.urop_relevance: Medium

part1_professor_database.name: Tommi Jaakkola
part1_professor_database.title: Thomas Siebel Professor, EECS and IDSS

research_groups.name: NLP Group (co-lead)
research_groups.focus: Statistical inference, generative models, NLP for computational chemistry

part1_professor_database.research_summary: Co-leads MIT CSAIL NLP Group. Work spans statistical inference, generative models (PFGM++), conformal language modeling, and cooperative rationalization. His methods underlie modern approaches to scalable inference relevant to LLM training.
key_papers_2024_2025: PFGM++: generative model advances
Conformal language modeling
Learning Diffusion Models with Flexible Representation Guidance (NeurIPS 2025)
NLP for computational chemistry and functional genomics
part1_professor_database.urop_relevance: Medium

part1_professor_database.name: David Sontag
part1_professor_database.title: Professor, EECS

research_groups.name: Clinical ML Group
research_groups.focus: Clinical NLP with LLMs, zero-shot clinical extraction, collaborative LLMs

part1_professor_database.research_summary: Clinical NLP with LLMs. Developed zero-shot clinical information extraction using GPT-style models. Co-authored Co-LLM (combining general and expert LLMs for improved factuality).
key_papers_2024_2025: Co-LLM: combining general and expert LLMs
Zero-shot clinical information extraction using LLMs
LLMs for deciphering clinical notes and extracting medication regimens
part1_professor_database.urop_relevance: Medium

part1_professor_database.name: Leslie Kaelbling
part1_professor_database.title: Panasonic Professor, EECS

research_groups.name: Learning and Intelligent Systems (LIS)
research_groups.focus: LLMs for hierarchical robot planning, embodied AI, foundation models for decision-making

part1_professor_database.research_summary: Uses LLMs for hierarchical robot planning. Developed HiP framework where an LLM serves as the language reasoner in a three-pronged planning system. Integrates foundation models into embodied decision-making.
key_papers_2024_2025: HiP: Hierarchical Planning with LLM language reasoner for robot manipulation
Foundation models for embodied decision-making
Language-guided robot planning and task decomposition
part1_professor_database.urop_relevance: Medium

part1_professor_database.name: Daniela Rus
part1_professor_database.title: Andrew & Erna Viterbi Professor; CSAIL Director

research_groups.name: Distributed Robotics Lab
research_groups.focus: LLMs for robot task generation, generative AI for robotics, whale communication analysis

part1_professor_database.research_summary: CSAIL Director integrating generative AI into robotics. Lab uses LLMs for robot task generation (GenSim), applies language models to sperm whale communication analysis. Co-founded LiquidAI.
key_papers_2024_2025: GenSim: LLMs for robot task generation
Whale communication analysis with language models (Nature Communications 2024)
LGA: Language-Guided Abstraction for robot planning (with Shah)
part1_professor_database.urop_relevance: Medium

part1_professor_database.name: Julie Shah
part1_professor_database.title: Professor; Head, AeroAstro Department

research_groups.name: Interactive Robotics Group
research_groups.focus: Language-guided robot planning, human-robot collaboration, LLM-driven task abstraction

part1_professor_database.research_summary: Co-authored LGA (Language-Guided Abstraction) framework that uses natural language to help LLMs create planning representations for robotics. Bridges language models and embodied AI.
key_papers_2024_2025: LGA: Language-Guided Abstraction for robot planning (CoRL 2024)
ALGAE: Adaptive Language-Guided Abstraction from Contrastive Explanations (with Bobu)
Preference-Conditioned Language-Guided Abstraction (HRI 2024)
part1_professor_database.urop_relevance: Medium

part1_professor_database.name: Andreea Bobu
part1_professor_database.title: Assistant Professor, AeroAstro (joined July 2024)

research_groups.name: CLEAR Lab
research_groups.focus: LLMs for robotics and human-robot interaction, VLM-guided robot learning

part1_professor_database.research_summary: Uses LLMs extensively for robotics and human-robot interaction. Developed ALGAE (Adaptive Language-Guided Abstraction from Contrastive Explanations, CoRL 2024). Uses LLMs to infer task goals from open-ended dialog. MGAIC-funded project on VLM-guided robot learning augmentation.
key_papers_2024_2025: ALGAE: Adaptive Language-Guided Abstraction from Contrastive Explanations (CoRL 2024)
LLMs for inferring task goals from open-ended dialog
VLM-guided robot learning augmentation (MGAIC)
Designing Jet Engines with AI: Analyzing Human-LLM Collaboration
part1_professor_database.urop_relevance: VERY HIGH - new lab, has active ELX UROP listing, MGAIC-funded

part1_professor_database.name: Vikash Mansinghka
part1_professor_database.title: Principal Research Scientist, BCS / CSAIL

research_groups.name: Probabilistic Computing Project
research_groups.focus: Probabilistic programming for LLM control, self-steering language models, generative AI for databases

part1_professor_database.research_summary: Developed LLaMPPL, a programming language for controlling language models central to the DisCIPL framework. Co-authored work on self-steering language models and GenSQL (generative AI for databases).
key_papers_2024_2025: LLaMPPL: programming language for controlling language models
DisCIPL: self-steering language models framework
GenSQL: generative AI for databases
part1_professor_database.urop_relevance: Medium

part1_professor_database.name: Michael Cafarella
part1_professor_database.title: Principal Research Scientist, CSAIL

research_groups.name: Data Systems Group
research_groups.focus: LLM reasoning, data science agents, log-augmented generation

part1_professor_database.research_summary: Co-authored Log-Augmented Generation (scaling test-time reasoning in LLMs, ICLR 2026) and OpenEstimate (evaluating LLMs on reasoning under uncertainty, ICLR 2026). Works with Andreas on LLM reasoning systems.
key_papers_2024_2025: Log-Augmented Generation: scaling test-time reasoning in LLMs (ICLR 2026)
OpenEstimate: evaluating LLMs on reasoning under uncertainty (ICLR 2026)
Palimpzest: declarative AI workloads (with Kraska)
part1_professor_database.urop_relevance: Medium

part1_professor_database.name: Aude Oliva
part1_professor_database.title: Senior Research Scientist; MIT Director, MIT-IBM Watson AI Lab

research_groups.name: Oliva Lab / MIT-IBM Watson AI Lab
research_groups.focus: LLM fine-tuning, vision-language models, parameter-efficient methods

part1_professor_database.research_summary: Published Trans-LoRA (NeurIPS 2024), a data-free transferable parameter-efficient finetuning method for LLMs. Supervised UROP students on data-free LLM fine-tuning. Also works on vision-language models (ConMe, NeurIPS 2024).
key_papers_2024_2025: Trans-LoRA: data-free transferable parameter-efficient LLM finetuning (NeurIPS 2024)
ConMe: Vision-Language Models (NeurIPS 2024)
part1_professor_database.urop_relevance: HIGH - has supervised UROPs on LLM fine-tuning

part1_professor_database.name: Martin Rinard
part1_professor_database.title: Professor, EECS

research_groups.name: Rinard Research Group
research_groups.focus: LLM world models, program repair with LLMs, simulations of reality in LLMs

part1_professor_database.research_summary: Discovered simulations of reality developing deep within LLMs, indicating understanding beyond simple mimicry. Works on LLM world models and program repair. Co-developed GenSQL with Mansinghka.
key_papers_2024_2025: LLMs develop their own understanding of reality as language abilities improve
GenSQL: generative AI for databases
part1_professor_database.urop_relevance: Medium

part1_professor_database.name: David Karger
part1_professor_database.title: Professor, EECS

research_groups.name: Haystack Group
research_groups.focus: LLMex project, AI-based memory prosthetics, information management with LLMs

part1_professor_database.research_summary: Co-leads the LLMex project implementing Vannevar Bush's Memex vision using LLMs - building AI-based memory prosthetics that use LLMs to help individuals track information and proactively suggest relevant content.
key_papers_2024_2025: LLMex: Implementing Memex vision with LLMs
AI-based memory prosthetics with language models
part1_professor_database.urop_relevance: Medium

part1_professor_database.name: Joshua Tenenbaum
part1_professor_database.title: Professor of Computational Cognitive Science

research_groups.name: Computational Cognitive Science Group
research_groups.focus: Neurosymbolic LLM approaches, rational AI agents, common-sense reasoning

part1_professor_database.research_summary: Bridges cognitive science and AI. Co-leads major work on neurosymbolic approaches (LILO, Ada, DisCIPL) combining LLMs with symbolic planning. Studies LLMs for building rational agents and common-sense reasoning. MGAIC-funded 'Rational Generative AI' project.
key_papers_2024_2025: DisCIPL: self-steering language models (with Andreas, Mansinghka)
LILO: Language-inspired library of operators
Rational agents that explore and act like people (ICLR 2026)
Language and experience: computational model of social learning (ICLR 2026)
Loose LIPS: Battleship with language-informed program sampling (CogSci 2024)
MGAIC Rational Generative AI project
part1_professor_database.urop_relevance: Medium

part1_professor_database.name: Roger Levy
part1_professor_database.title: Professor, BCS; Chair of MIT Faculty (2025-2027)

research_groups.name: Computational Psycholinguistics Lab
research_groups.focus: Computational linguistics, LLM evaluation, LLM world knowledge, language processing

part1_professor_database.research_summary: Central to computational linguistics and NLP. Published LINC (combining LLMs with first-order logic provers, EMNLP 2023), studies prompting vs. probability measurements in LLMs, and evaluates LLM world knowledge (EWoK). Extensively collaborates with Andreas, Kim, Solar-Lezama.
key_papers_2024_2025: LINC: Combining LLMs with first-order logic provers (EMNLP 2023)
EWoK: Evaluating basic world knowledge in language models
Prompting vs. probability measurements in LLMs
Learning phonotactics from linguistic informants (ICML 2024 Oral, with Andreas)
part1_professor_database.urop_relevance: Medium

part1_professor_database.name: Patricia (Pattie) Maes
part1_professor_database.title: Germeshausen Professor of Media Arts and Sciences

research_groups.name: Fluid Interfaces Group
research_groups.focus: LLMs for mixed reality, wearable AI memory, LLM agents, cognitive enhancement

research_groups.name: AHA (Advancing Humans with AI) Program
research_groups.focus: Human-AI collaboration research

part1_professor_database.research_summary: One of the most active Media Lab faculty in LLM research. LLMR (LLM for Mixed Reality, CHI 2024), Memoro (wearable AI memory using LLMs), studies of LLM agent behavior vs humans (NeurIPS 2024), RAG-driven multimodal LLMs for ECG, LLM-based language learning chatbots.
key_papers_2024_2025: LLMR: LLM for Real-time Prompting of Interactive Worlds (CHI 2024 Honorable Mention)
Memoro: Wearable AI memory assistant using LLMs
How LLM agents deviate from human choices (NeurIPS 2024)
RAG-driven multimodal LLMs for ECG interpretation
LLM-based language learning chatbots for growth mindset
Generative AI Creative Applications (course)
part1_professor_database.urop_relevance: HIGH - has ELX UROP (AttentivU), but that specific one is BCI-focused

part1_professor_database.name: Ramesh Raskar
part1_professor_database.title: Associate Professor; Associate Director, MIT Media Lab

research_groups.name: Camera Culture Group
research_groups.focus: LLM agent benchmarking, multimodal data selection

research_groups.name: NANDA (Networked Agents and Decentralized AI)
research_groups.focus: Internet of AI Agents, agent-to-agent protocols, decentralized AI infrastructure

part1_professor_database.research_summary: PI of NANDA, building foundational infrastructure for an 'Internet of AI Agents' extending Anthropic's MCP and Google's Agent-to-Agent protocols. Also leads LLM agent benchmarking and multimodal data selection.
key_papers_2024_2025: NANDA: Algorithms to Unlock The Internet of AI Agents
Agent-based models (multiple AAMAS 2023-2025 papers)
Differentiable agent-based modeling
LLM agent benchmarking frameworks
part1_professor_database.urop_relevance: VERY HIGH - has active ELX UROP on LLM Agent Benchmarking

part1_professor_database.name: Deb Roy
part1_professor_database.title: Professor of Media Arts and Sciences

research_groups.name: Center for Constructive Communication
research_groups.focus: LLMs for public narrative understanding, NLP for social systems, generative AI for communication

part1_professor_database.research_summary: Extensive NLP and LLM research: LLMs for Public Narrative Understanding (NAACL 2025), LLM Targeted Underperformance (AAAI 2026), LLMs for legal concept learning (ACL 2024), LLM speech summarization. Teaches 'Generative AI for Constructive Communication.'
key_papers_2024_2025: LLMs for Public Narrative Understanding (NAACL 2025)
LLM Targeted Underperformance Disproportionately Impacts Vulnerable Users (AAAI 2026)
Leveraging LLMs for Learning Complex Legal Concepts through Storytelling (ACL 2024)
LLM-based speech summarization (Interspeech 2024)
part1_professor_database.urop_relevance: Medium

part1_professor_database.name: Cynthia Breazeal
part1_professor_database.title: Professor; MIT Dean for Digital Learning; Associate Director, Media Lab

research_groups.name: Personal Robots Group
research_groups.focus: LLM/GenAI in social robotics, empathy in LLMs, AI tutoring

research_groups.name: MIT RAISE
research_groups.focus: Responsible AI for education

part1_professor_database.research_summary: Uses LLM/generative AI in social robotics for long-term interactions. Published HEART-felt Narratives (EMNLP 2024) on empathy in LLMs. MGAIC-funded project on AI-driven calculus tutoring.
key_papers_2024_2025: HEART-felt Narratives: empathy in LLMs (EMNLP 2024)
Foundation models for medical AI
AI-driven calculus tutoring (MGAIC)
part1_professor_database.urop_relevance: Medium-High

part1_professor_database.name: Pat Pataranutaporn
part1_professor_database.title: Assistant Professor of Media Arts and Sciences (2025)

research_groups.name: Cyborg Psychology Group / NeuraFutures
research_groups.focus: LLM psychology, false memories from AI, generative AI for education, AI-human interaction

part1_professor_database.research_summary: Prolific researcher at AI-psychology intersection. False memory formation from LLM chatbots (IUI 2025), overtrust of AI-generated medical advice (NEJM AI 2025), FutureYou (AI version of future self), generative AI for vocabulary learning (CHI 2024). Co-director of AHA program.
key_papers_2024_2025: False memory formation from LLM chatbots (IUI 2025)
Overtrust of AI-generated medical advice (NEJM AI 2025)

part2_kept_elx_urops.title: AI + Chaotic System
part2_kept_elx_urops.overview:
This project explores AI methods for chaotic systems, which are inherently unstable: even a tiny perturbation in initial conditions can lead to dramatically different outcomes, fundamentally limiting predictability. Our goal is to develop foundational machine learning models that serve as universal representations for ergodic systems, with applications in weather forecasting, climate engineering, and extreme event prediction.

Large-scale simulations in fields such as turbulence and lithography often involve millions to billions of mesh points. Capturing these multi-scale phenomena accurately is challenging, as traditional single-scale models struggle with consistency across scales. To address this, we aim to build a multi-scale foundation model that leverages the principle of consistency—a property of many PDEs in which solutions on larger domains, when restricted to subdomains, align with solutions computed directly on those subdomains. By incorporating this principle, our approach has the potential to dramatically improve the efficiency, accuracy, and generalizability of data-driven PDE solvers. Beyond enabling robust generalization across different scales, this research raises a deeper question: Can we uncover emergent behaviors that manifest only beyond the training scale?

Responsibilities:
- Conduct literature reviews on modern AI/ML approaches in science.
- Design and implement machine learning models.
- Deploy models on GPU/TPU clusters.
- Validate models on large-scale scientific datasets.
- Collaborate with the research team and participate in group meetings.

Preferred Skills?
- Previous search experience in machine learning and generative models.
- Proficiency in PyTorch (required); experience with JAX preferred.
- Mathematical maturity (numerical analysis, partial differential equations). 
- Background in computational fluid mechanics and partial differential equations preferred.
- Additional knowledge in earth, atmosphere and ocean science is a plus.


Additional Information?
- Applicants must apply for course credit.
- This UROP has the possibility of extending into the winter and spring.
- To apply, please submit a CV and transcript to Zongyi Li (zongyili@mit.edu).

part2_kept_elx_urops.title: Agentic AI for Streamlining the Power Grid Interconnection Process
part2_kept_elx_urops.overview:
Project Background:
 The U.S. electric grid is facing unprecedented interconnection backlogs, with more than 2,600 GW of generation and storage projects waiting in queues nationwide. Under FERC Order 2023 and related reforms, grid operators (ISOs/RTOs) have transitioned to cluster-based studies, yet bottlenecks remain due to repeated restudies, document-heavy workflows, heterogeneous modeling requirements, and coordination overhead.
This project explores how Large Language Models (LLMs) and multi-agent AI systems can streamline critical parts of the interconnection process. The goal is to develop an agentic workflow composed of structured LLM agents (prompt parsing, data curation, planning/optimization, and output handling) that:
•	Parse developer project descriptions into structured datasets
•	Integrate them with grid modeling data (PTDFs, network topology, generator fleets)
•	Solve simplified deliverability and network upgrade screening models
•	Provide decision-oriented summaries and cost proxies
The current prototype is implemented in Python using a multi-agent framework and includes optimization-based screening for transmission overloads under worst-case dispatch scenarios. The goal is to expand this into a robust, modular decision-support platform for developers and planners.
part2_kept_elx_urops.prerequisites:
Required:
•	Strong Python programming skills
•	Experience building structured software systems (not just scripts)
•	Interest in LLM-based systems and agentic workflows
Highly Preferred:
•	Experience with agent frameworks (e.g., AutoGen, LangChain, CrewAI, etc.)
•	Exposure to optimization (linear programming, mixed-integer programming)
•	Experience with UI frameworks (PySide6, Streamlit, etc.)
Beneficial but Not Required:
•	Exposure to power systems, energy systems, or electricity markets

part2_kept_elx_urops.title: Building Interactive AI Agents for Biological Data Exploration in Mantis
part2_kept_elx_urops.overview:
Join our team in creating advanced AI agents that enhance the Mantis platform—a cutting-edge tool designed to revolutionize how researchers interact with complex biological data. Mantis integrates structured and unstructured datasets into dynamic, interactive visualizations, offering a versatile environment for data-driven discovery. In this project, you will help develop AI agents that provide real-time data orchestration, computational augmentation, and interactive, context-aware insights to guide researchers through high-dimensional biological landscapes.
Key Features of Mantis and AI Agents Integration:
Narrator Agent: Provides real-time, contextual explanations of data, guiding researchers through complex visualizations and highlighting key biological concepts, patterns, and insights. The agent uses advanced NLP models, including GPT-based systems, fine-tuned on scientific literature to interpret and narrate data exploration paths.
Orchestrator Agent: Manages complex tasks by decomposing them into subtasks distributed among specialized agents, coordinating outputs, and fusing them into unified, actionable insights. The Orchestrator automates background processes, continuously monitors data for significant patterns, and dynamically adjusts workflows based on user interactions.
Sandbox Integration: The agents are integrated with Mantis’s Sandbox environment, a Jupyter-like computation tool that supports real-time analysis, statistical testing, and visualization. The agents trigger and manage computational tasks such as differential expression analysis and structural mapping directly within the Sandbox, providing seamless transitions between visualization, computation, and interpretation.
Responsibilities:
Agent Development: Design and implement the Narrator and Orchestrator agents using machine learning frameworks like PyTorch and TensorFlow, developing algorithms for real-time data interpretation, task decomposition, and integration of outputs across diverse data types.
Computational Enhancement: Work on integrating AI-driven computations with the Sandbox environment, ensuring smooth execution of analyses, dynamic feedback loops, and real-time updates that guide users through data exploration.
Adaptive Learning Modules: Develop adaptive learning capabilities for agents, allowing them to refine their interactions based on user feedback and research context, using reinforcement learning pipelines and context-aware adaptation techniques.
Automated Insights and Alerts: Implement systems for continuous data monitoring and anomaly detection, enabling the agents to provide automatic insights and recommendations, enhancing the platform’s role in supporting dynamic, data-driven hypothesis testing.
Skills Required:
Technical Proficiency: Strong programming skills in Python, with experience in machine learning libraries such as PyTorch, TensorFlow, or Scikit-learn. Proficiency in NLP frameworks like Hugging Face Transformers is highly desirable for developing interactive, narrative-driven agents.
Data Science and Visualization: Familiarity with data visualization tools (e.g., Matplotlib, Plotly, PyMOL) and computational techniques, including graph neural networks, contrastive learning, and dimensionality reduction (e.g., t-SNE, UMAP).
Biological Data Integration: Knowledge of multi-modal data integration, computational biology, and the ability to handle high-dimensional datasets. Familiarity with knowledge graphs, co-expression networks, and perturbation analysis is a plus.
Collaborative Mindset: Strong problem-solving skills, attention to detail, and the ability to work collaboratively in a fast-paced, interdisciplinary environment. Clear communication skills are essential for documenting work and contributing to methodological discussions.
Expected Impact:
By joining this project, you will be at the forefront of developing next-generation AI agents that transform how researchers interact with and interpret biological data. Your contributions will help drive the integration of dynamic, real-time insights and computational capabilities, setting new standards for AI-assisted research environments.
How to Apply: Submit your CV, your transcript, a brief statement of interest highlighting relevant experience in computational biology, data visualization, or software development, and any past projects demonstrating your skills to Professor Manolis Kellis (kellis-admin@mit.edu). Join us in shaping the future of interactive computational environments for biological discovery!

part2_kept_elx_urops.title: Building an Interactive AI Agents Compute Augmentation platform for Biological Data Exploration and Quantitative Integration
part2_kept_elx_urops.overview:
This project is dedicated to creating a versatile data science workbench that integrates advanced visualization, AI-driven analysis, and interactive computation within a unified platform. A central feature of this aim is the Sandbox Environment, a Jupyter-like computational tool that allows users to dynamically explore biological data, perform custom analyses, and develop novel insights directly within the Mantis cognitive maps. This project aims to empower researchers by providing a seamless interface for real-time, data-driven hypothesis testing, linking computational outputs with interactive visualizations to foster a more intuitive and impactful research experience.
Key Responsibilities:
Develop Interactive Computational Tools: Design and implement computational modules within the Sandbox, enabling users to perform advanced analyses on selected data subsets. These modules will support tasks such as differential gene expression testing, pathway enrichment analysis, structural comparisons, and other quantitative biological computations, integrated directly into the Mantis environment.
Custom Visualization Integration: Work with visualization libraries such as PyMOL, nglview, Py3Dmol for 3D protein structure rendering; Matplotlib, Seaborn, Plotly for gene expression and single-cell data visualizations; and Scanpy for advanced single-cell exploration. Develop customized visual outputs that link computational results with visual representations, enhancing the interpretability of complex datasets.
AI-Driven Contextual Exploration: Integrate AI-powered agents that provide contextual narrative insights and interpret computational results. These agents will interact with users, answering questions in natural language about the data and underlying models, providing an intuitive entry point for exploring complex biological phenomena.
Dynamic Analysis and Real-Time Hypothesis Testing: Implement features that allow users to dynamically adjust computational parameters, refine analyses, and visualize the impact of changes in real-time. This includes building interfaces for generating on-the-fly statistical plots, calculating pathway scores, and overlaying quantitative results onto interactive maps, creating a responsive and flexible environment for biological discovery.
Interactive Model Integration and Plugin Development: Support the integration of custom models and plugins into the Sandbox, allowing users to bring in specialized tools developed within Mantis or their own computational workflows. This feature will enable seamless exploration of custom latent embeddings, structural models, and other advanced datasets directly within the platform, supporting a diverse range of research applications.
Ideal Candidate Profile:
Skills and Background: Proficiency in Python and experience with computational tools such as Jupyter notebooks, data visualization libraries, and machine learning frameworks (e.g., PyTorch, TensorFlow). Familiarity with biological data types, statistical analysis, and computational modeling is a plus. Prior experience with interactive data visualization, plugin development, or computational biology software will be highly valuable.
Learning Opportunity: Engage in hands-on development of an innovative computational environment at the intersection of AI, machine learning, and biological research. Gain experience working with advanced visualization tools, interactive AI agents, and cutting-edge data science methods, contributing to a platform designed to transform how researchers explore and interpret complex biological data.
Research Environment: Collaborate within a multidisciplinary team of computational biologists, software engineers, and AI researchers. This UROP offers a unique opportunity to develop and refine computational tools that directly impact scientific discovery, bridging the gap between data analysis, visualization, and interactive exploration.
Why Join? This UROP provides an unparalleled opportunity to work on a highly innovative project that is redefining the landscape of biological research. By contributing to the development of the Mantis Sandbox, you will play a crucial role in creating a platform that empowers researchers to explore data in new ways, bridging computational and biological insights in real time. Your work will directly enhance the platform’s capabilities, making advanced data science accessible to the broader research community.
How to Apply: Submit your CV, your transcript, a brief statement of interest highlighting relevant experience in computational biology, data visualization, or software development, and any past projects demonstrating your skills to Professor Manolis Kellis (kellis-admin@mit.edu). Join us in shaping the future of interactive computational environments for biological discovery!

part2_kept_elx_urops.title: Designing Jet Engines with AI: Analyzing Human-LLM Collaboration
part2_kept_elx_urops.overview:
Generative AI is changing how we approach engineering, but how do people actually interact with these systems over sustained periods? This UROP aims to answer that by evaluating students’ usage of Large Language Models (LLMs) during complex engineering tasks.

You will be working with a first-of-its-kind longitudinal dataset collected during IAP, tracking student teams as they interacted with LLMs to design and build gas turbine engines.

As a UROP on this project, your core responsibilities will include:

Qualitative Analysis: Performing thematic analysis on a large text corpus to synthesize and categorize AI usage characteristics.

Quantitative Analysis: Utilizing text analysis tools to measure conversational fluency and other engagement metrics over time.

Trend Identification: Uncovering interesting patterns, behavioral shifts, and changes in how students collaborate with AI over the course of the 4-week build.


Required Skills:
-Strong analytical thinking and attention to detail.
-Proficiency in Python or R for data processing and basic text analysis.

Recommended Skills:
- Familiarity with qualitative research methods (e.g., thematic analysis, qualitative coding).
- Experience with data visualization (e.g., Matplotlib, Seaborn, ggplot) to communicate trends over time.

part2_kept_elx_urops.title: Digital Frontlines
part2_kept_elx_urops.overview:
Since the full-scale Russian invasion of Ukraine in 2022, Russian propaganda has pervasively extended into frontline territories through identity-based influence operations (IOs). This research examines how the media content of Russian IOs spreads on Ukrainian Telegram channels and how it impacts people's sense of identity. We focus specifically on visual media—its rhetoric, participatory reproduction, and the role of bots—to understand how propaganda spreads across different channels and what makes certain messages more viral than others. The goal is to deepen our understanding of how visual content shapes public opinion and identity during wartime.

Concretely, the UROP will gain hands-on experience working with Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs). Informed by the team’s strong foundations in psychology, social science, and AI engineering, the UROP will contribute to advancing methods for feature representation, abstraction development, and structuring AIML data pipelines to push the boundaries of visual-oriented semantic reasoning in AI.

The research also offers the UROP an opportunity to:
- Join an international collaboration of researchers from leading disinformation research labs (e.g., Cambridge Social Decision-Making Lab, Center for an Informed Public, etc.)
- Co-author results for high-impact publications
- Extend the UROP into a Summer ‘26 overseas MISTI internship (Oslo)

part2_kept_elx_urops.title: Interactive Idea Navigator Collaborative Space: AI-Enhanced Team Interaction and Collaboration
part2_kept_elx_urops.overview: The future of work is rapidly evolving with the advent of deep learning and artificial intelligence (AI) technologies. This one-year undergraduate research project aims to investigate the potential impact of AI-powered productivity and collaboration tools, new modes of human-machine collaboration, and human-machine interaction incorporating uncertainty, trust, and behavioral biases. The research will involve exploring and developing novel approaches to enhance productivity and collaboration in the workplace using deep learning techniques. This includes gamification of tasks to enhance motivation and engagement, summarization of meetings and emails for efficient information digestion, word-clouds and task-lists for better visualization of goals, and incorporating social aspects into task lists to encourage collaboration and team bonding. The research will also involve investigating new modes of human-machine collaboration, such as typing autocomplete with multiple suggestions to foster creativity, beam search of conceptual options for idea completion, and personalization of AI systems based on individual and team history. Additionally, the research will explore human-machine interaction incorporating uncertainty, trust, and behavioral biases by building trust through explicit AI intentions and processes, visualizing knowledge embeddings and relationships among concepts, and integrating AI systems with various platforms for augmented interactions and seamless user experience. The findings of this research will contribute to understanding the potential of deep learning in shaping the future of work and human-machine interactions, and may have implications for improving productivity, collaboration, and creativity in various professional settings. This research project will provide an opportunity for the undergraduate student to gain hands-on experience in deep learning, human-computer interaction, and AI applications in the workplace, and contribute to the field of AI-driven technologies for work and collaboration.

part2_kept_elx_urops.title: LLM Agent Benchmarking & Multi-Modal Data Selection
part2_kept_elx_urops.overview:
As Large Language Models (LLMs) evolve into autonomous AI agents capable of complex task execution, static benchmarks/leaderboards fail to capture how these AI systems will perform in real world scenarios. Our research group at the MIT Media Lab is working on building  infrastructure to rigorously evaluate agents via adaptive interviews and improve them via high-value data selection. We are seeking UROPs to co-design and implement components of these automated evaluators in collaboration with PhD students and postdocs.

You have the choice to work on two technical tracks. In the Agent Benchmarking track, you will help build the AI interviewer system that adaptively grades an agent based on task completion probing. In the Multi-Modal Data Valuation track, you will focus on scalable data selection strategies to improve LLM/RLHF post-training by extending existing algorithms that quantify the value of  image-text data points.

This UROP is a good fit if you have experience with or interest in:
*    Working with LLM Agent APIs and tool-use frameworks.
*  Writing PyTorch code and managing multi-GPU training workflows.
*  Researching data-centric machine learning (data valuation, benchmarking).
*  Building reproducible and scalable systems (Docker, sandboxing).
*  Designing protocols for a novel "interview" evaluation paradigm.
part2_kept_elx_urops.prerequisites: We are looking for students with strong coding skills (Python, Bash, Git, PyTorch, LLM APIs) and familiarity with machine learning (e.g. 6.790, 6.7960 or equivalent experience).

part2_kept_elx_urops.title: Latent Lab: Reimagining the File System with AI
part2_kept_elx_urops.overview:
The Viral Communications group at the MIT Media Lab is looking for students excited to challenge one of the oldest metaphors in computing: the desktop. Our sub-project, Filescape, extends Latent Lab (https://www.media.mit.edu/projects/latent-lab/overview/) into a new kind of file system - one that organizes and visualizes content associatively rather than hierarchically.

We already have a working system, but we’re seeking collaborators to help push it further. The goals are twofold:

Technical Development – Extend support to additional file types and refine our semantic/visual organization pipeline.

User Studies – Design and run comparative studies that test Filescape against emerging AI-assisted file navigation interfaces such as MCP file servers and Apple Intelligence Spotlight.

If you’ve ever imagined a world where your files feel less like folders in a cabinet and more like living knowledge maps, this project is for you.

Relevant Disciplines:
- Human-Computer Interaction (HCI)
- User Interface & User Experience Design
- AI and Machine Learning (embeddings, NLP, clustering)
- Web Development and Visualization (React, JavaScript/TypeScript, WebGL)
- Backend Development (Python, FastAPI, database systems)
- Cloud & Compute Infrastructure (GCP, CI/CD, containerized environments)

Who We’re Looking For:
We value both builders and researchers:

- Builders who want to extend system capabilities (e.g., parsing/embedding new file formats).

- Researchers who want to evaluate how Filescape stacks up against chat- and search-based alternatives.

Application Requirement:
To express interest, please email Kevin Dunnell at dunnell@mit.edu. Your email must include examples of projects you’ve built (class projects, personal projects, or otherwise). Messages without examples will not receive a response.

part2_kept_elx_urops.title: Programming by Example for Agentic AI
part2_kept_elx_urops.overview:
How can we make the computational power of programming more accessible to nontechnical people?

Programming by Example (PBE) is a technique for instructing computers how to perform procedures, by having a person demonstrate the steps on concrete examples. The system records the steps, and can replay them on a different example. This is like a so-called "macro recorder", but the user can also instruct the system to generalize the procedure, so it can work in cases that are different from, but analogous to, the original examples. The user is cast in the role of a teacher, and the computer is a student that learns best by example. 

In the past, PBE has been hampered by the inability of the PBE agent to fully access the functionality of applications and the operating system on the computer. Now, though, Agentic AI is allowing LLMs to access the underlying capabilities of the apps and OS, via facilities like OpenAI's GPT Agent and Anthropic's Computer Use. Could these facilities, combined with the "vibe coding" of conversational LLMs, enable a new style of end-user programming that would be accessible to beginning and occasional users?

Please contact Henry Lieberman, henry@csail.mit.edu

part2_kept_elx_urops.title: Real-Time Generative AI for Musical Improvisation - UROP
part2_kept_elx_urops.overview:
The Responsive Environments group at the MIT Media Lab is working with multiple professional artists (most recently, GRAMMY-winning visiting artist Jordan Rudess) to develop real-time generative AI agents that can engage in live musical improvisation. We are looking for highly motivated and experienced UROPs, with extensive experience in Machine Learning and Generative AI to help us develop the project. Potential projects include:

- Developing optimized local AI pipelines for real-time processing (using optimized ML frameworks such as ONNX or GGML)

- Developing RL training methods to train new jam_bot models (using rule-based rewards and reward models trained on human feedback)

- Training new jam_bot models with additional input modalities (e.g., audio, gesture)

To apply or learn more about the project, contact lancelot@media.mit.edu

part2_kept_elx_urops.title: Tangible AI
part2_kept_elx_urops.overview: Join us in developing Tangible AI interactions. Sketchnotes are visual thoughts in motion—what if they could inspire the next generation of innovators? We are currently working on an interface that turns old sketchnotes into interactive, idea-sparking tools using a custom-trained AI. We’re looking for a curious, motivated student to help us prototype, test, and expand this human-AI co-ideation platform. If you are interested in shaping interactions across time and are passionate about creativity, AI, and design, please reach out!

part2_kept_elx_urops.title: World Models for Robotics from Human Videos
part2_kept_elx_urops.overview:
The main bottleneck in the path to general purpose robotics is real world embodied data. Two frontier research directions are world modelling and human pose estimation. Together, these two tasks solved properly can turn Youtube videos into a simulator, useful for training robots through reinforcement learning. 

In this project, you will train a simulator for manipulation from human videos. You will investigate state of the art practices for compute-efficient video training (eg see Test time training Tianyuan Zhang), and apply them to human videos.  We have already prepared one of the first large-scale human datasets with clean head poses and hand poses, ready to be ingested by a video training pipeline. You will be guided towards papers to read and assisted with algorithm development and technical issues. The ideal candidate has taken a machine learning course, is comfortable with linear algebra. A bonus is expertise with computer vision and robotics (you will need to use kinematics and basic computer vision models), as well as Pytorch programming expertise.

part2_eliminated_elx_urops.title: AI and the Future of Work

elx_only_entries.title: Digital Frontlines
elx_only_entries.overview:
Since the full-scale Russian invasion of Ukraine in 2022, Russian propaganda has pervasively extended into frontline territories through identity-based influence operations (IOs). This research examines how the media content of Russian IOs spreads on Ukrainian Telegram channels and how it impacts people's sense of identity. We focus specifically on visual media—its rhetoric, participatory reproduction, and the role of bots—to understand how propaganda spreads across different channels and what makes certain messages more viral than others. The goal is to deepen our understanding of how visual content shapes public opinion and identity during wartime.

Concretely, the UROP will gain hands-on experience working with Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs). Informed by the team’s strong foundations in psychology, social science, and AI engineering, the UROP will contribute to advancing methods for feature representation, abstraction development, and structuring AIML data pipelines to push the boundaries of visual-oriented semantic reasoning in AI.

The research also offers the UROP an opportunity to:
- Join an international collaboration of researchers from leading disinformation research labs (e.g., Cambridge Social Decision-Making Lab, Center for an Informed Public, etc.)
- Co-author results for high-impact publications
- Extend the UROP into a Summer ‘26 overseas MISTI internship (Oslo)
